lessons.title: Number Patterns & Sequences
lessons.key_formula: Arithmetic: add constant. Geometric: multiply constant

lessons.title: Basic Algebra
lessons.key_formula: Whatever you do to one side, do to the other
lessons.mnemonic: BALANCE: keep both sides equal always

lessons.title: Speed, Distance & Time
lessons.example: Average speed A→B→A at 60 & 40 km/h = 2×60×40÷(60+40) = 48 km/h
lessons.mnemonic: SDT Triangle: cover what you want to find

lessons.title: Probability
lessons.key_formula: P = Favourable ÷ Total. AND = multiply. OR = add
lessons.mnemonic: AND = multiply down. OR = add across

lessons.title: Work Rate & Pipes
lessons.example: Pipe A in 6h, Pipe B in 4h together: 1/6 + 1/4 = 5/12 → fills in 2.4h

lessons.title: Watson-Glaser: Inference
lessons.key_formula: TRUE → PROB TRUE → INSUFF DATA → PROB FALSE → FALSE
lessons.example: Revenue up, profit down → costs probably rose (Probably True, not True)
lessons.mnemonic: ONLY what the text says. NO outside knowledge.

lessons.title: GMAT Data Sufficiency
lessons.key_formula: Test Stmt 1 alone → Stmt 2 alone → Both together
lessons.example: Is x > 0? S1: x²=4 (x=±2, not enough). S2: x+2>0 (x>-2, not enough). Together: x=2. Answer: C
lessons.mnemonic: 1-2-C-E-D: the DS answer order to always check

lessons.title: Critical Reasoning
lessons.key_formula: Weaken = alternative cause or reverse causation
lessons.example: To weaken 'X causes Y': show Z causes Y, or Y causes X
lessons.mnemonic: ATACC: Assumption, Then Alternative Cause, Counter

lessons.title: Mixtures & Alligation
lessons.key_formula: Substance quantity stays constant when you add solvent
lessons.example: 40L at 25% acid + xL water = 30% acid → 10/(40+x)=0.3 → x=13.3L

lessons.title: Watson-Glaser: Arguments
lessons.key_formula: Strong = relevant + logical reason. Weak = emotional/anecdotal/irrelevant
lessons.example: 'My uncle's business failed' = weak anecdotal argument against minimum wage

daily_lectures.title: The Percentage Trap
daily_lectures.hook: Why 50% off then 50% on does NOT get you back to the original price
daily_lectures.content:
Most students think +10% then -10% = 0%. Wrong. It equals -1%. Here's why:

If your salary is ₦100,000:
→ +10% = ₦110,000
→ -10% of ₦110,000 = ₦99,000

You lost ₦1,000! This is because the second percentage applies to a DIFFERENT base.

The formula: (1 + r₁) × (1 - r₂) - 1 = net change
(1.10) × (0.90) - 1 = 0.99 - 1 = -0.01 = -1%

⚡ EXAM TIP: Never add percentages. Always multiply.

daily_lectures.title: Why Rates Add, Not Times
daily_lectures.hook: The single most common mistake in pipe and work problems
daily_lectures.content:
Student mistake: 'Pipe A takes 6h, Pipe B takes 4h, together they take 5h (average).'

This is WRONG. Here's why:

Pipe A fills 1/6 of the tank per hour.
Pipe B fills 1/4 of the tank per hour.
Together: 1/6 + 1/4 = 2/12 + 3/12 = 5/12 per hour.
Time = 12/5 = 2.4 hours.

RATES add. TIMES do not.

⚡ MNEMONIC: Think of them as workers earning money per hour — two workers together earn the sum of their rates.

daily_lectures.title: Average Speed Is Never the Arithmetic Mean
daily_lectures.hook: The harmonic mean — why your commute average isn't what you think
daily_lectures.content:
If you drive Lagos to Ibadan at 60 km/h and return at 40 km/h, your average speed is NOT 50 km/h.

Why? You spend MORE time at the slower speed, so it drags the average down.

Formula: Average speed = 2×v₁×v₂ ÷ (v₁+v₂)
= 2×60×40 ÷ 100 = 4800÷100 = 48 km/h

This is called the HARMONIC MEAN.

⚡ TRICK: The harmonic mean is always LESS than the arithmetic mean for two different speeds.

daily_lectures.title: Watson-Glaser: The Golden Rule
daily_lectures.hook: One rule that unlocks 80% of Watson-Glaser questions
daily_lectures.content:
THE RULE: Only accept conclusions that MUST follow from the given information. Nothing more.

Do NOT use:
❌ Your general knowledge
❌ Common sense assumptions
❌ 'Probably' or 'usually'

Only use:
✅ What the passage explicitly states
✅ What MUST logically follow

Example: 'Revenue grew 40%, profit fell 5%'
Can we conclude: costs rose? → PROBABLY TRUE (not certain — could be tax)
Can we conclude: the company is failing? → INSUFFICIENT DATA

⚡ When in doubt → choose Insufficient Data

daily_lectures.title: The Elimination Method
daily_lectures.hook: How to score 70% accuracy even on questions you don't know
daily_lectures.content:
Smart test-takers don't just answer — they eliminate.

STEP 1: Read the question and identify what TYPE of answer you need (a number? a direction? a logical conclusion?)

STEP 2: Immediately rule out any option that:
- Is the wrong type (e.g., negative when you need positive)
- Is impossibly large or small
- Contradicts the question data

STEP 3: From the remaining 2, apply your formula or logic.

Example: 'Average speed' question → eliminate arithmetic mean options immediately (always wrong for this type).

⚡ On a 4-option question, eliminating 2 wrong ones gives you 50/50 odds minimum.
